reason: This is an educational presentation or conference lecture, not an FLS task performance video. The frames show slides from the 45th ASMIUA conference (September 2022) featuring Dr. Ho Yee Tiong discussing 'Getting into Laparoscopic Surgery: The Learning Curve.' Content includes learning curve theory, training models (synthetic, 3D-printed, animal, live porcine), validation data, technology-enhanced learning, and proctorship/mentorship frameworks. No actual FLS task (Peg Transfer, Pattern Cut, Endoloop, or Suture tasks) is being performed.

teaching_content.presentation_title: Getting into Laparoscopic Surgery: The Learning Curve
teaching_content.speaker: Ho Yee Tiong, Senior Consultant Urology, Associate Professor Surgery, National University Hospital, Singapore
teaching_content.event: 45th Annual Scientific Meeting of Indonesian Urological Association (ASMIUA), Makassar, South Sulawesi, Indonesia, 14-18 September 2022
demonstrated_techniques: Learning curve concept introduction (Wright's 1936 aircraft manufacturing principle applied to surgical training)
Validated FLS dry lab exercises: Peg Transfer, Pattern Cut, Suturing & Knot Tying
Training modalities: SAGES (Fundamentals in Laparoscopic Skills), AUA (Basic Laparoscopic Urological Surgery), EAU (E-Basic Laparoscopic Urological Skills), and validated dry lab sets
Synthetic models and 3D printing for procedure-specific training (e.g., kidney models for partial nephrectomy)
Live animal labwork (porcine models) for hands-on surgical training with real tissue
Robotic surgery simulation for intracorporeal vascular anastomosis
Technology-enhanced learning platforms: virtual reality, augmented reality, mixed reality, video analytics
Home-made and commercial laparoscopy training simulators
Tele-proctoring capability during COVID-19 pandemic using remote stations and ZOOM
verbal_cues: Most important aspect of shortening the learning curve: a good Proctor/Perceptor
Proctors provide education, guidance, tips & tricks, ensure safety, and build confidence
See One, Do One, Teach One principle for skill transmission
FLS performance in dry lab correlates with OSATS (Objective Structured Assessment of Technical Skills) in live porcine surgery
Validation of dry lab exercises confirms their utility for training urologists
Multiple different avenues and learning mindsets (vs. fixed mindset) shorten learning curve
Hardwork is important but greater than drills alone—a good Proctor/Perceptor is critical
Technology-enhanced learning and equipment upgrades aid competency development
common_errors_addressed: Inadequate proctorship leads to prolonged learning curves
Fixed mindset limits surgical skill acquisition; adoption of learning mindset enhances outcomes
Relying solely on simulator drills without expert guidance and mentorship is insufficient
Insufficient exposure to operative cases delays competency (tipping point: ≤28 cases for laparoscopic donor nephrectomy)
Inadequate intraoperative guidance increases complications; reduction noted after ~24 procedures in teaching setting
teaching_content.educational_focus: Surgical education framework, learning curve optimization, training modality validation, role of proctorship/mentorship in accelerating competency development

confidence_rationale: Content is unambiguously an educational conference presentation with slides, speaker narration, research data, training facility photos, and no actual FLS task performance. Classification is certain.
